content:
Most apps encrypt data in transit, forget it at rest, and never even think about data in use. Here’s where your secrets quietly walk out the door and how to actually stop it

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Data. Locked. Leaked.Let me start with an uncomfortable question.
You shipped HTTPS. You set up SSL on your domain, you saw the little padlock in the browser, and you mentally checked the box that says “security: done.”
So here’s the question: where is your users’ data right now, this exact second, and who can read it?
If your honest answer is “uh… in the database, I think?” then this article is for you. Because the padlock you’re so proud of protects exactly one slice of your data’s life, and ignores two much bigger ones. Most apps I’ve audited aren’t hacked. They’re leaking. Slowly, quietly, through doors the developers didn’t even know were open.
Let’s walk through those doors one by one.
The three states of data (and the two everyone forgets)
Every piece of data in your system lives in one of three states at any moment:

• Data in transit — moving across the network (browser → server, service → service).

• Data at rest — sitting in your database, object storage, backups, logs.

• Data in use — loaded into memory, being processed by your code.
Here’s the thing nobody tells junior developers: HTTPS only covers #1. That’s it. The moment a request lands on your server and gets decrypted, your “encryption” is over. Everything after that is plaintext unless you did something about it.
And almost nobody does something about it.
TLS is a tunnel, not a vault. It protects the journey, not the destination.
Let me show you exactly where each one leaks.
Leak #1: Data at rest — your database is a plaintext diary
Open your production database right now. Run a SELECT * on your users table.
Can you read the email addresses? The phone numbers? The support messages? The uploaded document contents?
If yes, congratulations — so can anyone who gets a copy of that database. And databases get copied all the time, in ways that have nothing to do with a movie-style “hacker breaking in”:

• A misconfigured S3 bucket holding nightly backups.

• A .env file with DB credentials accidentally committed to a public repo.

• A disgruntled employee with read access.

• A cloud provider subpoena you never hear about.

• A snapshot restored to a staging box with no auth.
In every single one of those cases, “we use HTTPS” does absolutely nothing. The attacker isn’t on the wire. They’re holding the disk.
People love to say “but the database has a password.” So does your front door, and you still wouldn’t tape your bank PIN to it. A credential is access control. It is not encryption. The instant that credential leaks — and credentials leak constantly — the data is wide open.
What actually helps:

• Field-level encryption for sensitive columns (emails, tokens, PII, message bodies). Encrypt before it hits the database, decrypt only when you genuinely need the plaintext.

• Encryption at rest at the storage layer (transparent disk encryption) as a baseline — but understand it only protects against stolen physical disks, not a leaked DB password. It’s the floor, not the ceiling.

• Separate your keys from your data. If your encryption keys live in the same database as the encrypted data, you’ve locked the safe and taped the key to the door. Use a KMS or a dedicated secrets manager.
Here’s the mental model in code. The naive version:
// What most apps do
await db.users.insert({
email: "aditya@example.com", // plaintext
ssn: "123-45-6789", // plaintext, lol
});Versus the version that survives a database leak:
// Encrypt sensitive fields before they ever touch storage
await db.users.insert({
email: encrypt(EMAIL_KEY, "aditya@example.com"),
ssn: encrypt(SSN_KEY, "123-45-6789"),
});Now a stolen database dump is a pile of ciphertext instead of a pile of identities. Same breach, radically different blast radius.
Leak #2: Data in use — your logs are confessing everything
This is the leak that gets senior engineers, not just juniors. Because it’s invisible.
You can be doing field-level encryption perfectly, TLS everywhere, keys in a vault — and still be hemorrhaging secrets through the single most boring file in your stack: the log.

Get Aditya Yaduvanshi’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.
Remember me for faster sign in

Picture this very normal line of code:
console.log("Incoming request:", req.body);Looks harmless. Now imagine req.body contains a password, a credit card, an auth token, or a user's private message. That secret just got written, in plaintext, to:

• Your application logs

• Your log aggregator (Datadog, CloudWatch, Logtail…)

• Possibly a third-party error tracker

• And the disk of whatever box happened to handle the request
You encrypted the database with surgical precision, and then casually printed the plaintext into a file that gets shipped to four external services with their own access policies you don’t control. This is the developer equivalent of locking your house and leaving the key under a doormat labeled “KEY.”
Real-world offenders I see constantly:

• Error trackers capturing full request payloads. A 500 error helpfully attaches the entire body — including the password the user just typed.

• Verbose logging left on in production “temporarily” eighteen months ago.

• Stack traces that print local variables holding decrypted secrets.

• Analytics events that pass an entire user object instead of an ID.
What actually helps:

• Redact at the boundary. Strip or mask sensitive fields before anything gets logged, not after.

• Allowlist, don’t blocklist. Don’t try to remember every secret field to hide — explicitly choose the few fields that are safe to log, and drop the rest.

• Treat logs as a security surface. Who can read them? For how long? Are they encrypted? Most teams can’t answer this.
// Don't log the whole object — pick what's safe
logger.info("Incoming request", {
userId: req.user.id,
route: req.path,
// body deliberately omitted
});Your logs should be useful for debugging and useless to an attacker. Right now, for most apps, it’s the exact opposite.
Leak #3: Data in transit — but the part you skipped
“Wait, I do have HTTPS, you literally said that covers transit.”
It covers the browser-to-server hop. But modern apps aren’t one server. They’re a swarm: API gateway → auth service → a couple of internal microservices → a queue → three different third-party APIs.
Quick gut check: are all of those internal hops encrypted? Or did someone decide that “it’s inside the VPC, it’s fine” and send plaintext between services?
“Inside the network” is not a security boundary. It’s a vibe. Cloud networks are shared, misconfigured, and routinely breached laterally — an attacker who gets a foothold on one internal box shouldn’t get a free wiretap on every service that talks to it.
And then there’s the third leak inside the leak: the third parties. Every external API you call — payment processor, email provider, analytics, that one AI endpoint — is another place your users’ data lands in plaintext, governed by their security, their retention policy, their breach history. You are exactly as secure as your least careful vendor.
What actually helps:

• Encrypt service-to-service traffic too (mTLS internally), not just the public edge.

• Minimize what you send to third parties. Does the analytics call really need the user’s email, or just an anonymous ID?

• Know your vendors’ retention. “We delete after 30 days” is a very different risk profile from “we keep it forever for model training.”
The pattern behind all three leaks
Step back and notice what these three have in common.
In every case, the data was protected somewhere and naked somewhere else. The encryption was treated as a feature you bolt onto one layer — the transport — instead of a property that follows the data through its entire life.
That’s the core mistake. Security isn’t a checkbox at the edge of your app. It’s a property of the data itself.
The gold standard here is end-to-end encryption — where data is encrypted on the client, stays encrypted as it travels, stays encrypted at rest, and is only ever decrypted by the people who are actually supposed to read it. Your server stores ciphertext. Your logs see ciphertext. Your database breach leaks ciphertext. The plaintext exists only at the two ends that matter.
That’s a much higher bar, and not every app needs full E2E for every field. But the direction is the point: stop thinking “how do I encrypt the connection” and start thinking “how do I make sure this specific secret is unreadable to everyone who shouldn’t read it, in every state it’s ever in.”
A 10-minute audit you can run today
Forget the theory. Go do this right now:

• SELECT * your most sensitive table. Can you read the secrets? If yes — that data leaks the moment the DB does.

• Grep your codebase for console.log / logger near request bodies, auth, or payment code. Find out what you're actually printing.

• Open your error tracker and look at one real captured error. What’s in the payload? Probably more than you’d like.

• List every third-party API you POST user data to. For each, answer: what exactly do they receive, and how long do they keep it?

• Check where your encryption keys live. If the answer is “next to the encrypted data,” fix that first.
If any of those made you wince — good. That wince is the most valuable security tool you own. Most breaches aren’t sophisticated. They’re a SELECT * on a database somebody forgot to encrypt, or a log file that wrote down a password.
The takeaway
HTTPS was never the finish line. It was the warm-up.
Your data lives in three states in transit, at rest, and in use and the padlock in the browser only guards one of them. The other two are where the quiet leaks happen: the plaintext database column, the over-eager log line, the internal hop nobody bothered to encrypt, the vendor with a sloppy retention policy.
You don’t have to fix all of it tomorrow. But you do have to see it. Run the audit. Find your leaks. Encrypt the data, not just the tunnel.
Because right now, your app is probably leaking secrets — and the whole problem is that you don’t know it.

If this saved you from a future incident report, leave a clap or two and follow — I write about the unglamorous engineering that actually keeps software alive. What’s the worst “we logged the password” story you’ve witnessed? Drop it in the comments.
